results.title: UKMTO Update: Volatile Security Environment Across Hormuz, Bab al Mandab and the Arabian Gulf
results.author: Dryad Global
results.text:
UKMTO Update: Volatile Security Environment Across Hormuz, Bab al Mandab and the Arabian Gulf

[Maritime Risk Intelligence Blog](https://channel16.dryadglobal.com/)

# UKMTO Update: Volatile Security Environment Across Hormuz, Bab al Mandab and the Arabian Gulf

Written by [Dryad Global](https://channel16.dryadglobal.com/author/dryad-global)| March 1, 2026 at 8:00 PM

On 01 March 2026, UKMTO issued Update 002 to Advisory 003-26, highlighting a highly volatile maritime security environment in the Arabian Gulf (also known as Persian Gulf), Gulf of Oman, North Arabian Sea, Bab al Mandab (BAM), and the Strait of Hormuz due to significant ongoing regional military activity.

This includes a substantial military presence contributing to elevated threats to commercial shipping, with risks of miscalculation or misidentification near military units, sensitive maritime facilities, and infrastructure. Mariners face potential for VHF hailing, directed communications from military units, and significant GNSS/electronic interference (including disruptions to AIS, positioning, navigation, and communications systems), which may be intermittent and unpredictable.

Claims of closure or transit restr
highlights:
On 01 March 2026, UKMTO issued Update 002 to Advisory 003-26, highlighting a highly volatile maritime security environment in the Arabian Gulf (also known as Persian Gulf), Gulf of Oman, North Arabian Sea, Bab al Mandab (BAM), and the Strait of Hormuz due to significant ongoing regional military activity.
[...]
This includes a substantial military presence contributing to elevated threats to commercial shipping, with risks of miscalculation or misidentification near military units, sensitive maritime facilities, and infrastructure. Mariners face potential for VHF hailing, directed communications from military units, and significant GNSS/electronic interference (including disruptions to AIS, positioning, navigation, and communications systems), which may be intermittent and unpredictable.
[...]
Claims of closure or transit restrictions in the Strait of Hormuz continue to circulate via open source reporting and VHF, but no official closure has been communicated through recognized channels such as NAVAREA warnings or IMO broadcasts; such VHF indications do not constitute a legal suspension of transit passage under UNCLOS. Broader regional instability extends threats beyond the Strait of Hormuz, including elevated tensions in the Bab al Mandab. JMIC guidance urges continuous VHF Channel 16 watch, AIS transmission per policy, adherence to Traffic Separation Schemes and corridors, professional responses to hails, enhanced risk assessments (considering military activity and insurance), and immediate reporting of unusual activity, suspicious approaches, interference, or incidents to UKMTO or other centers. The situation is under close monitoring with further updates expected as warranted.
results.summary:
Summary:
- The UKMTO/Advisory 003-26 Update (March 1, 2026) describes a highly volatile security environment across the Strait of Hormuz, Gulf of Oman, Bab al Mandab, and the Arabian Gulf, driven by significant ongoing military activity in the region.
- Key risks for watchstanders at the Strait of Hormuz:
  - Elevated threat of miscalculation or misidentification near military units and sensitive facilities.
  - Potential for direct military communications, VHF hails, and interference with GNSS, AIS, and other navigation and communications systems (intermittent and unpredictable).
  - Rumors of transit closures in the Strait of Hormuz, but no official closure or UNCLOS-based suspension issued through NAVAREA or IMO channels.
- Operational implications for watchstanders:
  - Maintain continuous VHF Channel 16 watch and AIS per policy.
  - Strict adherence to Traffic Separation Schemes and corridors; prepare professional responses to hails.
  - Conduct enhanced risk assessments that account for military activity, potential GNSS/electronic interference, and insurance considerations.
  - Report unusual activity, suspicious approaches, interference, or incidents promptly to UKMTO or equivalent centers.
- Broader regional instability (including Bab al Mandab) adds to the risk landscape and ongoing monitoring with updates anticipated as the situation evolves.
- Practical takeaway: maintain heightened situational awareness, verify communications, and follow JMIC guidance to mitigate collision, misidentification, and navigation risk while awaiting authoritative notices on any transit changes.

results.title: Iran allowing more ships through Strait of Hormuz, data suggest | US-Israel war on Iran News | Al Jazeera
results.author: John Power
results.text:
Iran allowing more ships through Strait of Hormuz, data suggest | US-Israel war on Iran News | Al Jazeera

Listen (3 mins)

Save

Share

Add Al Jazeera on Google

02:36

US-Israeli war on Iran disrupts global supply chains beyond oil and gas

Iran is allowing a small but growing number of commercial ships to pass through the Strait of Hormuz, according to ship tracking data.

Eight vessels, not including ships flying the Iranian flag, were detected in the critical waterway via the vessels’ automatic identification systems on Monday, maritime intelligence company Windward said on Tuesday.

## Recommended Stories

- [Larijani’s warning to US, Israel before his killing](https://www.aljazeera.com/video/newsfeed/2026/3/18/larijanis-warning-to-us-israel-before-his-killing)
- [Israel killed Larijani hoping to ‘torpedo’ chance of US-Iran talks](https://www.aljazeera.com/video/quotable/2026/3/17/israel-killed-larijani-hoping-to-torpedo-chance-of-us-iran-talks)
- [Families search for loved ones after deadly attack on Kabul hospital](https://www.aljazeera.com/video/newsfeed/2026/3/17/families-search-for-loved-ones-after-deadly-attack-on-kabul-hospital)
- [Pakistan ‘strongly’ rejects claim it
highlights:
Iran is allowing a small but growing number of commercial ships to pass through the Strait of Hormuz, according to ship tracking data.
[...]
Eight vessels, not including ships flying the Iranian flag, were detected in the critical waterway via the vessels’ automatic identification systems on Monday, maritime intelligence company Windward said on Tuesday.
[...]
The number of transits was “nearly double” the numbers seen in recent days, according to Windward.
[...]
Michelle Wiese Bockmann, an analyst at Windward, said that a growing number of ships have been rerouting via Iran’s territorial waters, suggesting that Tehran is allowing “permission-based transits to friendly countries”.
[...]
“Western-affiliated vessels won’t voluntarily come into Iranian waters, but likely Chinese, Indian and others will,” Bockmann said in a post on X.
[...]
MarineTraffic, another ship tracking service, recorded nine transits on Monday and Sunday, compared with five over the previous two days.
[...]
Traffic through the strait, which normally carries about one-fifth of global oil supplies, has plunged more than 95 percent since the start of the United States and Israel’s war on Iran.
[...]
Daily transits by non-Iranian ships, mostly Chinese, Indian and Pakistani-flagged vessels, have dropped into the single digits amid Iranian threats against shipping in the region.
[...]
The effective halt of traffic through the waterway has sent oil prices surging above $100 per barrel, an increase of more than 40 percent compared with before the start of the
[...]
Tehran has sent mixed messages about the status of the strait, which is bordered by Iran, the United Arab Emirates and Oman.
[...]
Iranian Minister of Foreign Affairs Abbas Araghchi said on Monday that the strait was “open, but closed to our enemies”, after a spokesperson for the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) earlier this month warned that any ship attempting passage would be set ablaze.
[...]
US President Donald Trump said on Tuesday that Washington did not need other countries’ help to unblock shipping traffic, even as he chided NATO partners for rebuffing his proposals for the deployment of an international coalition of warships to secure the waterway.
[...]
The US military said late on Tuesday that it had dropped bunker buster bombs on “hardened” Iranian missile sites located near the strait.
[...]
“The Iranian anti-ship cruise missiles in these sites posed a risk to international shipping in the strait,” US Central Command said in a post on X.
results.summary:
Summary:
- The Strait of Hormuz remains a critical chokepoint for global energy and maritime trade, with current data indicating a cautious gradual increase in non-Iranian ship transits through Iranian territorial waters.
- Windward reports eight vessels (excluding Iranian-flagged ships) transiting, nearly doubling recent days; MarineTraffic records nine transits over the weekend. This suggests Tehran may be permitting “permission-based” transits for friendly nations (e.g., China, India) while non-Iranian traffic remains highly constrained.
- Overall traffic through the strait has collapsed by over 95% since the US-Israel–Iran crisis intensified, contributing to elevated oil prices (regionally over $100/bbl) and heightened risk to global supply chains.
- The Iranian stance is mixed: officials describe the strait as “open, but closed to our enemies,” signaling continued deterrence against adversaries even as some commercial vessels navigate under restricted terms.
- U.S. and allied actions (air and cyber/strike activity on targets near the strait) continue to influence maritime risk, with potential implications for shipping routes, insurance costs, and port/terminal security in the Gulf.
- For watchstanding: monitor ongoing traffic patterns (non-Iranian transits, vessel types and flags), bilateral signaling from Tehran, and any changes in US/Coalition maritime patrol tempo or statements. Maintain awareness of potential escalations that could abruptly tighten or reopen passage, and assess ripple effects on oil supply routes, port security, and contingency routing for international commerce.

results.title: Strait of Hormuz Traffic Grinds to Near Halt as Security Threat Remains ‘Critical,’ Advisory Warns
results.author: Mike Schuler
results.text:
Strait of Hormuz Traffic Grinds to Near Halt as Security Threat Remains ‘Critical,’ Advisory Warns

FILE PHOTO: Tankers are seen off the coast of the Fujairah, as Iran vows to close the Strait of Hormuz, amid the U.S.-Israel conflict with Iran, in Fujairah, United Arab Emirates, March 3, 2026. REUTERS/Amr Alfiky/File Photo

# Strait of Hormuz Traffic Grinds to Near Halt as Security Threat Remains ‘Critical,’ Advisory Warns

Commercial shipping through the Strait of Hormuz has slowed to a near standstill as missile attacks, drone threats, and widespread electronic interference continue to [rattle the region](https://gcaptain.com/tag/iran-conflict/), according to the latest maritime security advisory from the Joint Maritime Information Center (JMIC).

In its latest update, JMIC said the regional maritime threat environment remains highly volatile amid the escalating conflict involving the United States, Israel, and Iran, warning that the risk to merchant shipping across the Arabian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and northern Arabian Sea remains “CRITICAL,” meaning an attack is considered almost certain.

While the strait technically remains open to navigation, vessel movements have collapse
highlights:
Commercial shipping through the Strait of Hormuz has slowed to a near standstill as missile attacks, drone threats, and widespread electronic interference continue to rattle the region, according to the latest maritime security advisory from the Joint Maritime Information Center (JMIC).
[...]
In its latest update, JMIC said the regional maritime threat environment remains highly volatile amid the escalating conflict involving the United States, Israel, and Iran, warning that the risk to merchant shipping across the Arabian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and northern Arabian Sea remains “CRITICAL,” meaning an attack is considered almost certain.
[...]
While the strait technically remains open to navigation, vessel movements have collapsed as shipowners and charterers reassess the risks of transiting one of the world’s most important maritime chokepoints.A review of vessel tracking data cited in the advisory shows that traffic through the waterway has nearly stopped.
[...]
“Recent AIS review indicates transits have reduced to single-digit levels, with only one confirmed commercial transit observed in the past 24 hours,” the advisory said.
[...]
Data from MarineTraffic.com shows tanker traffic through Strait of Hormuz down by more than 90%. “Unlike several other vessel segments where movements have largely ceased, some tankers are still traveling east and west through the strait, with a number of voyages occurring under AIS blackouts,” says Matt Wright, Principal Freight Analyst at Kpler, parent company to MarineTraffic.com.
[...]
A containership transiting the Strait
[...]

[...]
The JMIC advisory also highlighted widespread GPS and GNSS interference affecting vessels operating in the region, including AIS anomalies, positional inaccuracies, and degraded communications systems. Shipmasters were urged to closely monitor navigation equipment and be prepared to rely on radar, visual bearings, and traditional navigation techniques if satellite-based systems become unreliable.
[...]
that the maritime security environment could
[...]
missile activity expands closer to major shipping routes or if strikes occur against ports, offshore infrastructure
[...]
or terminals supporting Gulf energy exports
[...]
Shipping companies operating in the region have been advised to conduct enhanced voyage risk assessments before entering the Gulf, maintain reporting links with the United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations (UKMTO) center and the Maritime Security Centre – Indian Ocean (MSCIO), and follow established Best Management Practices for maritime security.
results.summary:
Summary:
- The Strait of Hormuz remains a high-risk, volatile maritime environment due to missile attacks, drone threats, and widespread GNSS/GPS interference tied to the US-Israel-Iran conflict.
- Operational reality: normal commercial traffic has collapsed to near standstill (down over 90%), with only occasional transits and some AIS blackouts; no formal closure declared.
- Security threat level: JMIC deems the risk to merchant shipping as “CRITICAL” (almost certain for an attack), requiring heightened watchstanding and risk assessment for all vessels transiting or intending to transit the strait.
- Navigation guidance for watchstanders: prepare for degraded satellite navigation; rely on radar, visual bearings, and traditional navigation methods as GNSS/GIS anomalies and communications degradation persist.
- Energy significance: the strait remains essential, handling about 20% of global oil consumption and a substantial LNG portion, amplifying the impact of any disruption on watchstanding decisions.
- Incident context: recent attacks on vessels (e.g., a containership hit by a projectile) underscore the ongoing threat environment and the necessity for robust threat awareness and defensive readiness.
- Recommendations for watchstanders: maintain heightened AIS vigilance, monitor GNSS status and potential anomalies, use alternative navigation methods proactively, and coordinate closely with ballast/watch teams and local authorities for route planning and contingency options.

results.title: Iran Strait of Hormuz Risk Advisory March 2026 - Maritime News
results.text:
Iran Strait of Hormuz Risk Advisory March 2026 - Maritime News

# Iran's Maritime Risk Increases; Strait of Hormuz Traffic Near Halt Amid Advisories

US MARAD, UKMTO and JMIC maritime advisories warn of high risks in the Persian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz and Gulf of Oman following over 20 confirmed incidents involving commercial vessels since late February. Transit volumes remain severely diminished while operators should coordinate with NCAGS while keeping a distance from naval units.

Maritime operators face heightened risks in the Persian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and Gulf of Oman as advisories from major authorities underscore ongoing disruptions to commercial shipping. Between March 8 and March 20, 2026, reports confirmed more than 20 incidents involving vessels and offshore facilities, contributing to near-paralyzed transit through key chokepoints.

## US MARAD Issues Advisory 2026-004 on March 16

The US Maritime Administration released Advisory 2026-004 on March 16, reinforcing high risk of attacks against commercial shipping in the Persian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and Gulf of Oman. Masters are instructed to maintain at least 30 nautical miles from US naval units, cooperate with
highlights:
US MARAD, UKMTO and JMIC maritime advisories warn of high risks in the Persian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz and Gulf of Oman following over 20 confirmed incidents involving commercial vessels since late February. Transit volumes remain severely diminished while operators should coordinate with NCAGS while keeping a distance from naval units.
[...]
Maritime operators face heightened risks in the Persian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and Gulf of Oman as advisories from major authorities underscore ongoing disruptions to commercial shipping. Between March 8 and March 20, 2026, reports confirmed more than 20 incidents involving vessels and offshore facilities, contributing to near-paralyzed transit through key chokepoints.
[...]
The US Maritime Administration released Advisory 2026-004 on March 16, reinforcing high risk of attacks against commercial shipping in the Persian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and Gulf of Oman. Masters are instructed to maintain at least 30 nautical miles from US naval units, cooperate with coalition queries, and coordinate transits with NAVCENT NCAGS. The advisory warns against complying with over-the-horizon VHF or email instructions from Iranian forces, advising vessels to log encounters and continue on track.
[...]
The Joint Maritime Information Center (JMIC), via UKMTO, elevated the maritime threat level to critical across the Persian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and Gulf of Oman. More than 20 incidents have occurred since February 28 escalations, including attacks on vessels, navigation system interference, and risks to port infrastructure. The risk of further disruptions remains high.
[...]
UKMTO has reported over 20 maritime incidents in and around the Strait since March 1. Operators are urged to monitor VHF 16, report abnormal activity, and stay aware of electronic interference and misidentification risks.
[...]
Commercial traffic through the Strait of Hormuz remains extremely limited. On March 15, only three outbound crossings were recorded, with no inbound transits, marking continued suppression far below normal levels. Maritime activity across the Gulf stayed heavily disrupted, distorting cargo flows and energy shipments.
[...]
Since the conflict's onset, 20 confirmed incidents targeted commercial vessels and offshore infrastructure, primarily cargo ships: 40 percent bulk carriers, 30 percent tankers, 20 percent container vessels, and 10 percent service vessels. About 45 percent of affected vessels had recent UAE port calls, with others from Iraq, Thailand, Vietnam, and Brazil.
[...]
, MARAD, and flag directives. Dual-theater risks combine Hormuz with Red Sea exposures, prompting route redist
results.summary:
Summary:
- The Strait of Hormuz and surrounding Persian Gulf are under elevated maritime security risk through March 2026, with authorities warning of continued attacks, navigation interference, and mine/missile/UAS threats.
- Key advisories: US MARAD Advisory 2026-004 (Mar 16, 2026) and earlier advisories warn masters to maintain at least 30 nm from US naval units, coordinate with NAVCENT NCAGS, log encounters, and avoid following over-the-horizon instructions. GNSS spoofing/jamming remains a navigation hazard.
- Threat level: UKMTO/JMIC raised the maritime threat level to critical for the region due to 20+ incidents since late Feb 2026, including attacks, navigation system interference, and risks to port infrastructure.
- Operational impact for watchkeepers: transit volumes through the Strait are severely reduced (e.g., March 15: only three outbound crossings, no inbound), with heightened risk of misidentification and electronic interference. Vessels should monitor VHF Ch.16, report abnormal activity, and maintain distance from naval units; expect extended vigilance for suspicious behavior, GPS anomalies, and potential boarding or seizure attempts.
- Port and area-specific notes: Some ports (e.g., Fujairah, RAK) report GPS anomalies and restrictions; Norwegian-flagged ships face elevated MARSEC/ISPS measures in the region. Mine-threat considerations and broader air-sea surveillance restrictions further complicate operations near energy hubs.
- Watchstanding relevance: 
  - Maintain safe spacing from naval forces and coordinate transit plans via NCAGS/ NAVCENT.
  - Prepare for possible delays, GNSS disruptions, and misidentification risks; implement anti-boarding and security procedures.
  - Continuously monitor for official advisories and ensure real-time communication with national/regional maritime authorities.

results.title: One month of war in the Gulf: Windward maps maritime disruption to date — SMI DIGITAL
results.author: Written By  Diptesh Chohan
results.text:
One month of war in the Gulf: Windward maps maritime disruption to date — SMI DIGITAL

# One month of war in the Gulf: Windward maps maritime disruption to date

1 Apr

Four weeks after the launch of Operation Epic Fury, the maritime system is no longer “reacting to disruption. It is operating within it,” says Maritime AI risk intelligence service Windward. The company summarises events to date as follows:

The first days of the conflict triggered an immediate collapse in commercial confidence. Tankers halted, [some] insurance coverage was withdrawn, and transit through the Strait of Hormuz fell to near zero. Vessel strikes, GPS jamming, and infrastructure attacks quickly expanded the risk environment across the Gulf and adjacent waters.

By the second week, this disruption had become systemic, Windward continues. Shipping routes began redistributing globally, energy exports were rerouted or reduced, and port operations started absorbing the pressure of displaced cargo flows.

By the third week, the picture began to evolve. The Strait was no longer simply inactive. Limited movement resumed under tightly constrained conditions. Selective transit became visible, with vessels routing
highlights:
Four weeks after the launch of Operation Epic Fury, the maritime system is no longer “reacting to disruption. It is operating within it,” says Maritime AI risk intelligence service Windward. The company summarises events to date as follows:
[...]
The first days of the conflict triggered an immediate collapse in commercial confidence. Tankers halted, [some] insurance coverage was withdrawn, and transit through the Strait of Hormuz fell to near zero. Vessel strikes, GPS jamming, and infrastructure attacks quickly expanded the risk environment across the Gulf and adjacent waters.
[...]
By the second week, this disruption had become systemic, Windward continues. Shipping routes began redistributing globally, energy exports were rerouted or reduced, and port operations started absorbing the pressure of displaced cargo flows.
[...]
By the third week, the picture began to evolve. The Strait was no longer simply inactive. Limited movement resumed under tightly constrained conditions. Selective transit became visible, with vessels routing through Iranian territorial waters, often following prior calls at Iranian ports. Activity remained extremely low, but no longer absent, indicating that access was being granted on a case-by-case basis rather than fully restricted.
[...]
By the fourth week, this model matured into a controlled system. Transit was no longer just selective, but actively managed. A permission-based corridor north of Larak Island became established, with staged and sequenced vessel movement. Throughput increased modestly, but access became more explicitly filtered, with certain vessels denied passage entirely.
[...]
Meanwhile, a drone strike targeted a laden VLCC – reportedly the Kuwaiti-flagged ‘Al Salmi’- approximately 31 nautical miles northwest of Dubai late on Monday 30 March. The vessel was carrying approximately 2 million barrels of crude at the time of the strike. The incident caused a fire onboard, which was later contained without casualties or reported pollution.
[...]
The event signals a shift in exposure, comments Windward. Risk is not limited to transit corridors. Anchored and staging vessels within Gulf waters are increasingly vulnerable to direct targeting.
results.summary:
Summary:
- The Gulf conflict has transformed the Strait of Hormuz from a transit bottleneck into a managed, permission-based corridor. In the first month, commercial confidence collapsed, insurance coverage tightened, and Hormuz traffic fell to near zero. 
- By week two, disruption became systemic: global route shifts, rerouted or reduced energy exports, and stressed port operations as displaced cargo accumulated.
- In week three, limited movement resumed under tight controls with selective transit through Iranian waters, often following prior calls at Iranian ports; access was case-by-case rather than total ban.
- By week four, a formal, controlled system emerged: a North-of-Larak permission-based corridor with staged vessel movements, modest throughput gains, and explicit filtering—some vessels denied passage entirely.
- Overall, Windward describes a new operating model under sustained geopolitical pressure: constrained, selective, and adaptive maritime trade through Hormuz.
- Additional risk signals emerged: a drone strike hit a laden VLCC near Dubai, illustrating that exposure extends beyond transit corridors to anchored and staged vessels within Gulf waters, increasing vulnerability to direct targeting and flashpoints for escalation.

Key security implications for Strait of Hormuz watchstanding:
- Expect ongoing, dynamic control measures rather than permanent bans; monitoring should emphasize permission criteria, corridor openings, and vessel eligibility.
- Heightened risk to anchored/staging assets near Hormuz, not just transit routes; security around port calls, anchorage, and support vessels is essential.
- Insurance and commercial confidence volatility may influence routing decisions, with potential for rapid shifts in traffic patterns and vessel scheduling.
- Intelligence indications of case-by-case access suggest heightened importance of up-to-date vessel compliance, credible route planning, and real-time risk assessments for entry into Iranian territorial waters or adjacent corridors.
- Preparedness should include contingency plans for sudden tightening or expansion of access, potential diversions, and coordination with port authorities, insurers, and fleet operators.

results.title: Iranian Shadow Fleet and Greek Affiliated Ships Lead Strait of Hormuz Transits
results.author: Lori Ann LaRocco
results.text:
Iranian Shadow Fleet and Greek Affiliated Ships Lead Strait of Hormuz Transits

FILE PHOTO: Tankers are seen off the coast of the Fujairah, as Iran vows to close the Strait of Hormuz, amid the U.S.-Israel conflict with Iran, in Fujairah, United Arab Emirates, March 3, 2026. REUTERS/Amr Alfiky/File Photo

# Iranian Shadow Fleet and Greek Affiliated Ships Lead Strait of Hormuz Transits

By Lori Ann LaRocco – Recent vessel tracking data details the continued movements of Iranian dark vessels as well as other ships moving through the Strait of Hormuz.

Lloyd’s List Intelligence reports in addition to the Iranian shadow fleet moving post war, Greek affiliated ships are outnumbering the number of vessels traversing the Strait.

“The Greek affiliated vessels are loaded with oil within the Middle East Gulf, and now sailing towards the Strait of Hormuz,” explained Bridget Diakun Senior Risk and Compliance Analyst, Lloyd’s List Intelligence. “This is really critical, because if they’re able to go through without incident, this might encourage some other owners and operators to change their calculation and perhaps take the risk of moving. But again, this kind of depends on how things pan out
highlights:
By Lori Ann LaRocco – Recent vessel tracking data details the continued movements of Iranian dark vessels as well as other ships moving through the Strait of Hormuz.
[...]
Lloyd’s List Intelligence reports in addition to the Iranian shadow fleet moving post war, Greek affiliated ships are outnumbering the number of vessels traversing the Strait.
[...]
“The Greek affiliated vessels are loaded with oil within the Middle East Gulf, and now sailing towards the Strait of Hormuz,” explained Bridget Diakun Senior Risk and Compliance Analyst, Lloyd’s List Intelligence. “This is really critical, because if they’re able to go through without incident, this might encourage some other owners and operators to change their calculation and perhaps take the risk of moving. But again, this kind of depends on how things pan out and how long they are left waiting as more time drags on.”
[...]
Diakun explained Iranian loadings are still underway and movements of the sanction ships in the shadow fleet ships show the additional precautions being taken.
[...]
“Beyond disabling their AIS, we spotted a sanctioned container ship that was coming inbound to the Gulf. It ended up drifting waiting for three or four days before actually making the transit,” she said.
[...]
Lloyd’s List defines a properly confirmed dark vessel is one that has an official offline and online AIS duration.
[...]
At the start of the crisis, Lloyds List said between 40 and 50 ships went offline, mostly in the Middle East Gulf, and some at regional ports. Diakun expects those figures to be revised upwards in the coming days.
[...]
transits.
[...]
On March 4, an Iranian linked containership and a dark transit of a shadow fleet tanker heading out towards the Gulf of Oman were captured by Lloyd’s List.
[...]
Kpler data show the Iranian tankers are in the Gulf and showing no signs of a flight response. The majority of the tankers remain in or around the Strait of Hormuz and UAE/Omani anchorage zones.
[...]
“Movements suggest caution, low speeds, clustering, some drifting, but not a suspension of activity,” said Dimitris Ampatzidis, Senior Risk & Compliance Analyst at Kpler. “There is no clear sign of widespread unloading inside the Gulf over the last couple of days, which would be atypical for these trades anyway. In general, it’s typical that most Iranian crude discharge happens further east via ship-to-ship transfers or in Asia.”
[...]
Approximately 44 to 45 transits have gone through the strait since the start of the month, according to Lloyd’s List calculations. That is an approximate decrease of 90 percent in tonnage from normal levels.
results.summary:
Summary:
- The Strait of Hormuz remains a focal point for maritime security amid ongoing Iran-related tensions and sanctions.
- Lloyd’s List Intelligence tracks a continuing presence of Iran’s “shadow fleet” (dark/offline vessels) transiting the strait, alongside Greek-affiliated ships and a few UAE/Chinese-linked vessels.
- A significant share of observed transits are “dark” (offline AIS), with about 80% of tracked transits in that category; confirmation requires ships to come back online.
- Movement patterns show slow speeds, clustering, drifting, and cautious behavior rather than continuous high-volume unloading inside the Gulf, suggesting restrained activity and strategic caution.
- Iran’s loadings are ongoing; many tankers remain near the Strait, UAE/Omani anchorage zones, and the Gulf, with transfers likely proceeding via ship-to-ship movements further east.
- The data indicate a marked drop in overall strait transits (roughly 44–45 transits this month, about a 90% decline from normal), which has implications for regional security calculations and the risk calculus for non-state actors and shipping owners.
- Analysts note uncertainty around a potential free-pass thesis for certain non-Iranian entrants (e.g., Chinese-affiliated ships) and emphasize that weathering sanctions and strategic delays influence decision-making.
- Security relevance for watchstanders: monitor for continued dark-vessel activity, potential attempts to bypass AIS, convoy movements around Hormuz, and the timing of any unexpected loading/unloading that could signal shifts in Iranian crude flows or sanctions evasion.

results.title: Only 77 ships crossed Strait of Hormuz so far this month amid escalating Mideast war: data - TRT World
results.text:
Only 77 ships crossed Strait of Hormuz so far this month amid escalating Mideast war: data - TRT World

LIVE TV

2 min read

Only 77 ships crossed Strait of Hormuz so far this month amid escalating Mideast war: data

The number is significantly lower than 1,229 passages between March 1 and 11 last year.

Share

The Callisto tanker sits anchored in Port Sultan Qaboos as the traffic is down in the Strait of Hormuz, in Muscat, Oman, on March 12, 2026. / Reuters

March 13, 2026

Only 77 ships have so far crossed the Strait of Hormuz in March as the Middle East war disrupts one of the world's most vital shipping routes, a maritime data firm has reported.

Lloyd's List Intelligence said on Friday most of these vessels belonged to the so-called "shadow fleet" — ships used to skirt Western sanctions and regulations, typically linked to Russia and Iran.

They are often ageing ships in poor condition, without proper insurance and with opaque ownership.

The 77 transits recorded so far this month compare with 1,229 passages between March 1 and 11 last year, according to Lloyd's List Intelligence.

Iran's Revolutionary Guards have all but [closed the Strait of Hormuz,](https://www.trtworld.com

Since the start of March, 20 commercial vessels, including nine oil tankers, have been attacked or reported incidents, according to the UK Maritime Trade Operations agency.
[...]
Meanwhile, the International Maritime Organization has confirmed 16 incidents in the area, including eight involving oil tankers.
[...]
Iran's new supreme leader Mojtaba Khamenei said on Thursday that "the lever of blocking the Strait of Hormuz must definitely be used."
[...]
The strategy is aimed at harming the global economy in order to exert pressure on the United States.
[...]
Iran, Greece and China lead limited Hormuz traffic
[...]
"Over half of the tankers and gas carriers going through are shadow fleets," said Bridget Diakun, a senior analyst at Lloyd's List Intelligence.
[...]
"These ships are really used to disruptions," so are more likely to attempt the passage, she added.
[...]
According to the firm, vessels affiliated with Iran account for 26 percent of passages through Hormuz, followed by Greece with 13 percent and China with 12 percent.
[...]
"The main takeaway here is that... Iran is still exporting," Diakun said.
[...]
Separately, AFP counted around 40 vessels that have crossed the Strait of Hormuz since the start of the conflict, considering only those that kept their AIS transponder — the automatic identification system — switched on.
[...]
, Iran officials
[...]
-escalation in
[...]
high-level call
results.summary:
Summary:
- March traffic through the Strait of Hormuz is markedly down, with only 77 ships so far this month, far below 1,229 passages by the same date last year, reflecting disruption from the escalating Mideast conflict.
- Lloyd’s List Intelligence attributes much of the reduced traffic to the so-called “shadow fleet”—older, poorly insured vessels used to evade sanctions, often linked to Russia and Iran.
- Iran’s Revolutionary Guards have effectively tightened control of the strait, a critical chokepoint where about 20% of global oil passes.
- Since March began, at least 20 commercial vessels (including 9 oil tankers) have been attacked or reported incidents in the area; the International Maritime Organization has logged 16 incidents (8 of which involve oil tankers).
- Analysts note that the shadow fleet’s involvement and sanction evasion tactics increase security and insurance risks, complicating watchstanding and navigation planning for operators and navies.
- The situation underscores heightened maritime security concerns for Hormuz, including potential for rapid shifts in corridor usability, increased threat to shipping, and greater need for vigilant traffic monitoring, incident response readiness, and sanctions enforcement in watchstanding activities.

# Dark ships and shadow fleets – what is crossing 'closed' Strait of Hormuz?

Iran's move to shut the Strait of Hormuz has brought shipping through the 24-mile stretch of water close to a standstill.

Only a handful of vessels have transited in recent days, many switching off their tracking systems or linked to "shadow fleets".

"They can't physically close a waterway of that size, but the threat is there", said Richard Meade, editor-in-chief of maritime risk company Lloyd's List Intelligence.

Mr Meade said Iran has a proven history of using everything from ballistic missiles to unmanned aerial and seaborne drones.

What is making it through?

Sky News' Data & Forensics team has focused on 13 ships that have transited the Strait of Hormuz between 2 and 9 March.

We know the real number of crossings is much higher, as some ships turn their tracking system off, a practice known as going "dark".

Data from marine analytics firm IMF Portwatch suggests that in normal times,

On 2 March, the data shows the ship did an almost U-turn at around 2pm UTC. It continued to retreat from the area until 4 March, finally returning to the Port of Salalah in Oman, heading to the Gulf of Kutch on 10 March, where it appeared "laded", heavily loaded.

results.summary:
Summary:
- The Strait of Hormuz, a 24-mile waterway, has seen severely reduced traffic since Iran threatened to close it, with many ships going “dark” (turning off trackers) or joining a so-called shadow fleet.
- Shadow fleet vessels are tanker/oil carriers carrying sanctioned cargo (Iran, Russia, Venezuela); eight of 13 large crossings (2–9 March) were linked to this shadow fleet, highlighting heightened illicit or sanctioned-activity risk.
- Crossings reported from diverse flags (US, Marshall Islands, Gibraltar, UAE, Bahamas, Panama, India) show broad international involvement and complexity in tracking and verification.
- Attacks and security incidents surged early March, with at least 17 attributed incidents affecting vessels in the region from 28 Feb to 11 Mar, including fires, suspected projectiles, and crew evacuations.
- Iranian capabilities and past patterns (ballistic missiles, drones, seaborne drones) contribute to persistent risk, even if a full closure is unlikely.
- For watchstanding, key implications include: increased need for real-time tracking and anomaly detection (to identify dark/ghost fleets), heightened risk assessment around sanctioned-cargo movements, vigilance for incident advisories near the Gulf and Strait of Hormuz, and readiness for contingency routing and rapid response to incidents in the area.

results.title: GPS spoofing is scrambling ships in the Strait of Hormuz | Scientific American
results.author: Deni Ellis Béchard
results.text:
GPS spoofing is scrambling ships in the Strait of Hormuz | Scientific American

March 12, 2026

5 min read

[Add Us On GoogleAdd SciAm](https://www.google.com/preferences/source?q=scientificamerican.com)

# Why ships in the Strait of Hormuz can’t trust their navigation screens

GPS spoofing is distorting vessel positions and deepening the risk in one of the world’s most important shipping lanes

By [Deni Elli
highlights:
# Why ships in the Strait of
[...]
When ships enter the Strait of Hormuz between Iran and the Arabian Peninsula, their navigation screens report impossible things. Supertankers circle over dry land. Cargo vessels cross through airports. Container ships drift through a nuclear power plant. In the two weeks since the U.S. and Israel launched attacks against Iran, thousands of vessels have experienced navigation interference in the Persian Gulf. Commercial shipping through the strait, which carries roughly 20 percent of the world’s oil, has nearly ground to a halt.
[...]
“I’m quite certain that it is the Iranians who are doing this spoofing,” says Todd Humphreys, a professor of aerospace engineering at the University of Texas at Austin. The spoofing doesn’t just interfere with ships’ own GPS; it also causes their automatic identification system, or AIS, to broadcast false locations, often showing ships tracing large circles even when they aren’t moving. Researchers used to jokingly call these “crop circles,” Humphreys says. He suspects the circles are simply a default movement pattern in certain low-cost spoofing devices available on the open market. “But neither the spoofer itself nor the actual ship is going in circles,” he says. The spoofer likely sits on a tall tower or a tethered balloon called an aerostat on the Iranian coast as it broadcasts signals.
[...]
The real danger in the Strait of Hormuz isn’t that ship captains can’t find their own positions. “A seasoned crew knows their equipment is compromised once they start seeing circular patterns indicating that they are on land when they’re not,” Humphreys says. They can fall back on visual cues—binoculars, radar decoupled from GPS, shoreline matching. But the critical problem is that every ship’s AIS beacon is also fed by its compromised GPS receiver. The false location, Humphreys says, “is picked up by AIS receivers across the coast. And that’s what other ships see.”
[...]
A single ship could probably navigate the strait, but on the average day, 130 to 150 vessels—many of them immense—transit the Strait of Hormuz, which is only 21 miles across at its narrowest point. But when a captain looks at electronic chart displays and sees hundreds of circling ships in false locations, they don’t know where the others are, how fast they are moving or what they are about to do. “It’s just chaos on your electronic chart display,” Humphreys says. “These are enormous ships that take kilometers to slow down and change direction. Very few captains would be willing to put a supertanker through that narrow strait.”
results.summary:
Summary:
- The Strait of Hormuz is facing GPS spoofing that corrupts ships’ navigation screens and AIS signals, causing vessels to report false positions and follow odd patterns (e.g., circles), elevating collision and misrouting risk in one of the world’s busiest and most strategic maritime corridors.
- Since mid-2025, spoofing—likely conducted from Iranian coastal assets (towers or aerostats)—has intermittently forced crews to rely on visual navigation rather than satellite tools, increasing watchstanding challenges for maritime security and operations.
- The disruption compounds other threats in the region (rocket/drone incidents, warfare spillover) and threatens about 20% of global oil shipments, making real-time situational awareness, robust GPS alternatives, and secure AIS integrity critical for watchstanders.
- Experts highlight that spoofing not only misleads ship GPS but also distorts AIS broadcasts, creating “crop circle” appearance in vessel tracks that may mask actual movements, complicating traffic management and interdiction/defense planning.
- For watchstanders, the implications are: heightened need for conventional navigation fundamentals, cross-checks with multiple data sources, enhanced monitoring of AIS integrity, and readiness to implement contingency routing and defecting to manual piloting when satellite data is unreliable.

results.title: Strait of Hormuz GPS Spoofing & AIS Manipulation
results.author: Dimitra Sotirchos
results.text:
Strait of Hormuz GPS Spoofing & AIS Manipulation 

By Saleem Khan, Chief Data & Analytics Officer – Pole Star Global

Since 5 March, Pole Star Global has tracked 3,396 vessels in and around the Strait of Hormuz. Roughly 1 in 15 of them – 231 ships – are exhibiting suspicious positional anomalies. “Jumping” implausible distances, going briefly “ghosted,” or reappearing in patterns consistent with GPS and AIS interference. This is no longer a story about isolated glitches; it’s a systematic, geographically clustered distortion of reality at sea.

At the same time, we are seeing a near-total cessation of commercial traffic through the Strait, and some ships leaving the region are broadcasting extraordinary “destinations” such as “China Owner and All Crew”, turning what should be a navigational field into a live political message: don’t shoot, we’re not your target.

Beyond the chokepoint, about 540 oil tankers carrying an estimated 314 million barrels are currently at sea with no fixed destination, listed simply as “awaiting orders” – effectively a $30 billion floating oil exchange waiting for a safe route and a safe buyer.

Pole Star Global fuses AIS, satellite detections, ownership,
highlights:
Strait of Hormuz GPS Spoofing & AIS Manipulation 

By Saleem Khan, Chief Data & Analytics Officer – Pole Star Global
[...]
Since 5 March, Pole Star Global has tracked 3,396 vessels in and around the Strait of Hormuz. Roughly 1 in 15 of them – 231 ships – are exhibiting suspicious positional anomalies. “Jumping” implausible distances, going briefly “ghosted,” or reappearing in patterns consistent with GPS and AIS interference. This is no longer a story about isolated glitches; it’s a systematic, geographically clustered distortion of reality at sea.
[...]
At the same time, we are seeing a near-total cessation of commercial traffic through the Strait, and some ships leaving the region are broadcasting extraordinary “destinations” such as “China Owner and All Crew”, turning what should be a navigational field into a live political message: don’t shoot, we’re not your target.
[...]
Beyond the chokepoint, about 540 oil tankers carrying an estimated 314 million barrels are currently at sea with no fixed destination, listed simply as “awaiting orders” – effectively a $30 billion floating oil exchange waiting for a safe route and a safe buyer.
[...]
Pole Star Global
[...]
analytics to help governments
[...]
from manipulation. Our data offers an unusually
[...]
how conflict,
[...]
, and digital
[...]
are reshaping global trade in
[...]
The image below shows intentional AIS manipulation by a vessel called the NV Aquamarine (in red trails). This vessel was travelling at an astounding 102 knots (188 km/hr) from the Strait to the Iran/Iraq border. This is impossible for a tanker that has a capacity to carry 350,000 barrels of oil worth $35M at current spot rates.
results.summary:
Summary:
- The Strait of Hormuz is experiencing widespread maritime security anomalies: roughly 1 in 15 vessels tracked show suspicious GPS/AIS behavior, including implausible jumps, brief ghosting, and reappearances in distorted patterns.
- This AIS/GPS interference coincides with an almost complete drop in commercial traffic through the chokepoint, while some ships cast politically charged destinations (e.g., “China Owner and All Crew”) to signal messages rather than navigation targets.
- Beyond the Strait, about 540 oil tankers with an estimated 314 million barrels are adrift with no fixed destination, effectively creating a large, high-value floating oil exchange waiting for safer routing.
- Pole Star Global uses AIS, satellite data, ownership, and behavioral analytics to distinguish malfunction from manipulation, highlighting how conflict, coercion, and digital deception are reshaping global trade in real time.
- Notable example: the NV Aquamarine showed deliberate AIS manipulation and an extreme speed (102 knots), illustrating potential tactical distortions that exceed normal operational capabilities for a tanker of that size.
- Security relevance for watchstanders: real-time indicators of GPS/AIS manipulation and neighboring-region uncertainty require heightened vigilance, cross-checking of tracking data, and awareness of potential coordinated interference aimed at disrupting routing, visibility, and signaling in and near the Strait.

results.title: Hormuz Active Crisis 5 Ships Hit Traffic Collapses | gCaptain
results.author: Mike Schuler
results.text:
Hormuz Active Crisis 5 Ships Hit Traffic Collapses | gCaptain

Oil tankers pass through the Strait of Hormuz, December 21, 2018. REUTERS/Hamad I Mohammed

# Strait of Hormuz Enters Active Crisis: Five Ships Hit, Traffic Collapses, Tanker Markets Brace for Historic Shock

The Strait of Hormuz has entered its most dangerous phase in years after a concentrated wave of missile and drone attacks struck five commercial vessels across the Gulf of Oman and UAE waters. With ports temporarily suspending operations, insurers reassessing war-risk exposure, and AIS data showing transits collapsing, the region’s maritime security environment has shifted from elevated tension to active crisis.

As of Sunday, the Joint Maritime Information Center (JMIC) has [elevated](https://www.ukmto.org/-/media/ukmto/products/update-002---001---jmic-advisory-note-28_feb_2026_final.pdf?rev=54f3ebd757e14bccb7e6ac9b62108bcb) the regional maritime threat level to CRITICAL after confirmed missile and drone attacks against multiple commercial vessels in the Gulf of Oman, the Musandam approaches, and UAE coastal waters — a designation indicating an attack is considered almost certain.

In just 24 hours, five vessels w
highlights:
The Strait of Hormuz has entered its most dangerous phase in years after a concentrated wave of missile and drone attacks struck five commercial vessels across the Gulf of Oman and UAE waters. With ports temporarily suspending operations, insurers reassessing war-risk exposure, and AIS data showing transits collapsing, the region’s maritime security environment has shifted from elevated tension to active crisis.
[...]
As of Sunday, the Joint Maritime Information Center (JMIC) has elevated the regional maritime threat level to CRITICAL after confirmed missile and drone attacks against multiple commercial vessels in the Gulf of Oman, the Musandam approaches, and UAE coastal waters — a designation indicating an attack is considered almost certain.

[...]

[...]

[...]
- Jebel Ali briefly suspended operations following an aerial interception that caused a fire before resuming limited activity.
- Duqm Port in Oman remains operational after two UAV strikes injured one worker.
- Ras Tanura Refinery in Saudi Arabia was reportedly shut following a drone strike and fire.
- Bahrain temporarily suspended port operations and pilotage services.
- Qatar’s Ras Laffan and Mesaieed ports reported significant GPS signal degradation.
[...]
Significant GNSS/GPS interference is being reported across the Strait’s approaches, compounding navigational risks amid heavy congestion and heightened aerial threats.
[...]
AIS tracking indicates transits through the Strait may have fallen to just 28 vessels over a 24-hour period — roughly an 80% decline from the historical daily average near 138 vessels.
[...]
Cargo transits dropped from 98 combined eastbound/westbound movements on February 28 to just 18 on March 1. Tanker movements fell even more sharply, from 50 combined transits to just three.
[...]
JMIC cautioned the drop may reflect a short-term pause rather than a structural halt, but insurers and operators are clearly reassessing risk.
[...]
“Insurance availability may now act as a primary gating factor for transit decisions independent of formal navigational closure,” JMIC noted.
[...]
Even absent an official blockade, the combination of aerial threat, port suspensions, insurance withdrawals, and GPS disruption is effectively constraining commercialadfad movement.
results.summary:
Summary:
- The Strait of Hormuz has entered an active crisis with a concentrated wave of missile and drone attacks targeting five vessels in the Gulf of Oman and UAE waters, elevating regional maritime threats to CRITICAL per JMIC.
- Impacts on watchstanding: heightened vigilance required across all vessel types; all merchant ships are at risk regardless of flag or nationality. Expect increased watchstanding for proximity to high-threat areas, monitoring for continued missile/drone activity, and reassessment of routing, speed, and harbor entry plans.
- Vessel safety and response: attacks on crude and product tankers (MKD VYOM, SKYLIGHT, HERCULES STAR) and a U.S.-flag chemical/products tanker (STENA IMPERATIVE) demonstrate broad risk to hull, propulsion, and crew. Expect strong emphasis on damage assessment, collision avoidance in congested sectors, and readiness for emergency response and towage.
- Operational disruption: multiple ports briefly suspended operations (Jebel Ali, Bahrain), with regional energy infrastructure under strain (Ras Tanura fire/drones, Duqm impacts) and GPS/GNSS interference reported, complicating navigation and piloting.
- Watchstanding considerations:
  - Treat the area as high-risk with potential for escalation; maintain CRITICAL threat posture.
  - Implement tight AIS/vessel tracking and maintain heightened situational awareness for nearby unidentified aerial/sea threats.
  - Review contingency plans for rapid rerouting, shelter-in-place at sea, and coordinated emergency response.
  - Monitor GNSS/GPS integrity; prepare alternative navigation methods and cross-check with shore-based aids.
  - Coordinate with local authorities and maritime information centers for real-time advisories and danger zones.
- Bottom line: The current crisis markedly raises maritime security risk in the Hormuz region, demanding vigilant, flexible, and well-coordinated watchstanding practices to protect vessels, crews, and critical energy transport routes.

results.title: Ships Hug Iran’s Coast in Emerging Hormuz Workaround
results.author: Mike Schuler
results.text:
Ships Hug Iran’s Coast in Emerging Hormuz Workaround

Vessels typically transit the narrow international channel (purple), but recent tracks (red) show ships rerouting along Iran’s coastline. Source: Windward Maritime AI™ Platform

# Ships Hug Iran’s Coast in Emerging Hormuz Workaround

New tracking data suggests “permission-based” transits may be reshaping one of the world’s most critical shipping lanes

A new routing pattern is emerging in the Strait of Hormuz as vessels begin sailing through Iranian territorial waters to exit the Persian Gulf—one of the clearest signs yet that shipping in the region is shifting toward a controlled, permission-based system.

According to maritime analytics firm [Windward](https://windward.ai/blog/bulk-carriers-find-new-route-out-of-hormuz-strait/), at least five bulk carriers were tracked over March 15–16 sailing eastbound along Iran’s coastline, well outside the narrow international navigation channel typically used for transits through the strait.

The vessels’ trajectories show ships hugging the Iranian coast rather than using the internationally recognized traffic separation scheme, effectively rerouting through Iranian territorial waters to
highlights:
A new routing pattern is emerging in the Strait of Hormuz as vessels begin sailing through Iranian territorial waters to exit the Persian Gulf—one of the clearest signs yet that shipping in the region is shifting toward a controlled, permission-based system.
[...]
According to maritime analytics firm Windward, at least five bulk carriers were tracked over March 15–16 sailing eastbound along Iran’s coastline, well outside the narrow international navigation channel typically used for transits through the strait.
[...]
The vessels’ trajectories show ships hugging the Iranian coast rather than using the internationally recognized traffic separation scheme, effectively rerouting through Iranian territorial waters to reach the Gulf of Oman.
[...]
The findings come as traffic through the strait—normally one of the busiest energy chokepoints in the world—has effectively collapsed amid escalating attacks on commercial shipping.
[...]
Fresh data from MarineTraffic underscores the scale of the disruption. Over the past three days, just 15 vessels transited the Strait of Hormuz, including 8 bulk carriers, 5 tankers, and 2 LPG carriers—a fraction of normal volumes.
[...]
The imbalance is even more striking if you look at the directions ships are traveling: approximately 87% of those transits were outbound, with only 13% entering the Gulf.
[...]
The data suggests the strait is functioning less as a two-way shipping lane and more as a limited exit corridor, with vessels already inside the Gulf gradually leaving while very few are willing to enter.
[...]
allowed to transit.
[...]
At the same time, U.S. authorities have warned that Iranian forces may attempt to contact vessels via radio or email to request voyage information or instruct course changes—communications that officials say could be used to verify identity, cargo, or destination.
[...]
While there is no confirmed evidence that Iran has physically blocked the strait—for example, through naval mining—the combination of credible threat, targeted attacks, and selective routing behavior appears to be achieving a similar effect.
[...]
The traditional international shipping lane is now largely avoided, while alternative routes inside Iranian waters are being used by a small number of vessels—many with prior commercial ties to Iran.
[...]
Western-affiliated ship
[...]
, compliance, and security risks
[...]
, India, and other
[...]
If the pattern holds, the Strait of Hormuz may be evolving from a globally accessible shipping corridor into a selectively managed maritime gateway, where passage depends on permission.
results.summary:
Summary:
- A new routing pattern near the Strait of Hormuz shows vessels, including bulk carriers and LPGs, increasingly using Iran’s territorial waters to exit the Persian Gulf, rather than the international traffic separation corridor. This indicates a shift toward a “permission-based” transit regime supported by Iran’s proximity and potential verification steps.
- Tracking data (Windward, MarineTraffic) from mid-March reveals at least five bulk carriers (plus LPGs) rerouting east of the standard channel, often calling at Iran’s Imam Khomeini port beforehand. The trend expands beyond previously observed corridors and relies on expanded routing parameters to detect.
- The trend aligns with earlier reports of vessels diverting toward Iran between Larak and Qeshm before exiting the Gulf, interpreted as a de facto verification checkpoint (EOS Risk Group). The corridor may be functioning as a controlled exit rather than a neutral, open passage.
- US and allied warnings highlight potential Iranian attempts to contact vessels via radio or email to request voyage information, which could serve identity, cargo, or destination verification as part of the screening process.
- Overall security relevance: the Hormuz regime is shifting from a two-way, neutral transit lane to a more controlled, permission-based system. This increases operational risk for ships and crews (communication attempts, potential miscommunication), raises the likelihood of episodic delays, and complicates risk assessment for ships in or approaching the strait. It also signals possible increases in state-influenced chokepoint control, with implications for navigation planning, insurance, and compliance monitoring in maritime security watchstanding.
